questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of nucleophilicity for the following compounds:

(I)     (II)     (III)
options: (II) > (III) > (I)
(I) > (II) > (III)
(I) > (III) > (II)
(III) > (I) > (II)
questions.solution:
The nucleophilicity of pyridine derivatives depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.

Electron-donating groups (EDG) increase the electron density on the nitrogen atom, thereby increasing nucleophilicity. Conversely, electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) decrease the electron density on the nitrogen atom, decreasing nucleophilicity.

In compound (I), the $-\text{CH}_3$ group acts as an EDG through $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects, making it the strongest nucleophile among the given compounds.

In compounds (II) and (III), the $-\text{NO}_2$ and $-\text{CN}$ groups act as EWGs through $-I$ and $-M$ effects. Since $-\text{NO}_2$ is a stronger electron-withdrawing group than $-\text{CN}$, it decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom to a greater extent.

Thus, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{C}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
Reaction of $p$-toluidine with acetic anhydride in the presence of pyridine yields $N$-(4-methylphenyl)acetamide (compound A). This acetylation protects the $-\text{NH}_2$ group and prevents polysubstitution.

Compound A undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution with $\text{Cl}_2$ and $\text{AlCl}_3$. The $-\text{NHCOCH}_3$ group is a stronger activating and ortho/para-directing group compared to the $-\text{CH}_3$ group. Since the para position is blocked, the incoming chlorine electrophile is directed to the position ortho to the $-\text{NHCOCH}_3$ group, forming 2-chloro-4-methylacetanilide (compound B).

Acidic hydrolysis ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$) of compound B cleaves the amide bond, regenerating the primary amine. The final major product C is 2-chloro-4-methylaniline, where the chlorine atom is ortho to the $-\text{NH}_2$ group.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:

$\text{Alkene product mixture}$

$\text{Alkene product mixture} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \text{Final product mixture}$

Which of the following compounds are present in the final product mixture?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{HCHO}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCHO}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCHO}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves Hofmann exhaustive methylation followed by Hofmann elimination, and finally reductive ozonolysis.

Step 1: The starting tertiary amine reacts with excess $\mathrm{CH_3I}$ to form a quaternary ammonium iodide. Subsequent treatment with moist $\mathrm{Ag_2O}$ replaces the iodide ion with a hydroxide ion, yielding the quaternary ammonium hydroxide:
$\mathrm{[CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-N^+(CH_3)_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3] OH^-}$

Step 2: Heating the quaternary ammonium hydroxide causes Hofmann elimination. The hydroxide ion abstracts a $\beta$-hydrogen to form an alkene. There are two different types of $\beta$-hydrogens available in the alkyl groups attached to the nitrogen:
1. In the propyl group ($\mathrm{-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}$), abstraction of a $\beta$-hydrogen gives propene: $\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2}$.
2. In the isobutyl group ($\mathrm{-CH_2-CH(CH_3)_2}$), abstraction of the $\beta$-hydrogen gives 2-methylpropene (isobutylene): $\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH_2}$.

Thus, the alkene product mixture contains both propene and 2-methylpropene.

Step 3: Reductive ozonolysis ($\mathrm{O_3} / \mathrm{Zn-H_2O}$) of the alkene mixture cleaves the double bonds to form carbonyl compounds:
$\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O_3} / \mathrm{Zn-H_2O}} \mathrm{CH_3CHO} + \mathrm{HCHO}$
$\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH_2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O_3} / \mathrm{Zn-H_2O}} \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3} + \mathrm{HCHO}$

The final product mixture contains acetaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH_3CHO}$), acetone ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}$), and formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$).

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following reagents can reduce nitrobenzene to aniline?
(I) $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni}$
(II) $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$
(III) $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{NaOH}$
(IV) $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$
options: (I), (II) and (IV)
Only (II)
(I) and (II)
(I), (II) and (III)
questions.solution:
Catalytic hydrogenation using $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni}$ reduces nitrobenzene to aniline.

Reduction with active metals in acidic medium like $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$ or $\mathrm{Fe} / \mathrm{HCl}$ also reduces nitrobenzene to aniline.

Reduction with $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{NaOH}$ (alkaline medium) yields hydrazobenzene.

Reduction with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ yields azobenzene.

Therefore, only (I) and (II) reduce nitrobenzene to aniline.

Answer: (I) and (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major organic product formed in the following reaction sequence.

(i)
(ii) $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, heat
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an example of Gabriel phthalimide synthesis.

In the first step, the nucleophilic phthalimide anion undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with benzyl bromide to form N-benzylphthalimide.

In the second step, acidic hydrolysis of N-benzylphthalimide with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$, $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ and heat cleaves the imide bonds.

This hydrolysis yields phthalic acid and benzylamine ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$) as the primary amine product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the correct decreasing order of their nucleophilicity:

I.
II.
III.
options: III > I > II
II > III > I
I > II > III
I > III > II
questions.solution:
Nucleophilicity depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for donation.

In piperidine (III), the nitrogen atom is $sp^3$ hybridized and its lone pair is localized. The lesser s-character of the $sp^3$ orbital means the lone pair is held less tightly by the nucleus, making it highly available for donation. Thus, it is the strongest nucleophile.

In pyridine (I), the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized. The lone pair is localized in an $sp^2$ orbital and is not part of the aromatic $\pi$ system. However, due to the higher s-character of the $sp^2$ orbital compared to $sp^3$, the lone pair is held more tightly by the nucleus, making it less available for donation than in piperidine.

In pyrrole (II), the lone pair on the nitrogen atom is present in a p-orbital and is delocalized into the ring to complete the aromatic $6\pi$ electron system. Because the lone pair is involved in aromaticity, it is highly unavailable for donation, making pyrrole the weakest nucleophile.

Therefore, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is III > I > II.

Answer: III > I > II

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:

$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CO}_2\mathrm{H} \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) 3-Methylaniline}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{SOCl}_2} \xrightarrow{\text{(iii) } \mathrm{LiAlH}_4\text{, then } \mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O}^+} \text{ ?}$
questions.solution:
Step 1: Acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COOH}$) reacts with thionyl chloride ($\mathrm{SOCl}_2$) to form acetyl chloride ($\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COCl}$).

Step 2: Acetyl chloride reacts with 3-methylaniline to form an amide. The nucleophilic $-\mathrm{NH}_2$ group of 3-methylaniline attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetyl chloride, eliminating $\mathrm{HCl}$ to yield N-(3-methylphenyl)acetamide.

Step 3: The amide is then reduced by lithium aluminium hydride ($\mathrm{LiAlH}_4$). $\mathrm{LiAlH}_4$ reduces the carbonyl group ($-\mathrm{C}(=\mathrm{O})-$) of the amide to a methylene group ($-\mathrm{CH}_2-$), converting the amide into an amine.

Thus, the $-\mathrm{NH}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group is reduced to an $-\mathrm{NH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3$ (ethylamino) group. The final major product is N-ethyl-3-methylaniline, which corresponds to the structure in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the incorrect statement among the following:
options: $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ cannot reduce the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group.
The order of the reduction reaction by $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ and that of the reduction by $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$ is two in each case.

In spite of a very small rate constant for the reduction of any carbonyl function other than aldehydes and ketones, the rate of reduction with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ becomes appreciable due to a large concentration of hydride ions.
The $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is more ionic than the $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{H}$ bond, and hence $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ can produce a larger concentration of hydride ions than $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ is a strong reducing agent that can reduce the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group. It reduces aliphatic nitro compounds to primary amines and aromatic nitro compounds to azo compounds. Therefore, the statement that $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ cannot reduce the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group is incorrect.

The reduction of carbonyl compounds by complex metal hydrides like $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$ follows second-order kinetics, being first order with respect to the substrate and first order with respect to the reducing agent.

Aluminum is less electronegative than boron, making the $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{H}$ bond more polar and ionic than the $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{H}$ bond. This allows $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ to act as a stronger hydride donor, producing a larger concentration of hydride ions. This higher concentration makes the rate of reduction appreciable even for less reactive carbonyl functions.

Answer: $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ cannot reduce the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{B}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reactant contains two amino groups: a secondary aromatic amine (part of the tetrahydroquinoline ring) and a primary aliphatic amine ($-\text{NH}_2$).

The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom of the secondary aromatic amine is delocalized into the benzene ring due to resonance, making it less nucleophilic. On the other hand, the primary aliphatic amine has a localized lone pair, making it significantly more nucleophilic.

When treated with 1 equivalent of acetaldehyde ($\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$), the more nucleophilic primary aliphatic amine selectively attacks the carbonyl carbon to undergo nucleophilic addition followed by elimination of water, forming an imine (Schiff base) as intermediate A:
$\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} \rightarrow \text{R-N=CH-CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

In the next step, intermediate A is treated with $\text{Sn} + \text{HCl}$, which acts as a reducing agent. It reduces the carbon-nitrogen double bond of the imine to a single bond, converting it into a secondary amine:
$\text{R-N=CH-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Sn/HCl}} \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ (or $\text{R-NHC}_2\text{H}_5$)

The secondary aromatic amine in the ring remains unaffected throughout the reaction sequence. Thus, the final product B has an ethylamino group ($-\text{NHC}_2\text{H}_5$) in place of the original primary amine.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of nucleophilicity for the following amines:

(i)

(ii)

(iii)
options: (i) > (ii) > (iii)
(ii) > (iii) > (i)
(iii) > (i) > (ii)
(i) > (iii) > (ii)
questions.solution:
Nucleophilicity of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair on the nitrogen atom for attack on an electrophile. Steric hindrance plays a major role in determining nucleophilicity; greater steric crowding around the nitrogen atom significantly decreases its nucleophilicity.

In compound (i) (quinuclidine), the alkyl groups are constrained in a rigid bicyclic ring system. This structural feature "ties back" the alkyl chains, exposing the lone pair on the nitrogen atom. Due to minimal steric hindrance, it is a highly effective nucleophile.

In compound (ii) (triethylamine), the three ethyl groups are flexible and can rotate, creating moderate steric hindrance around the nitrogen atom, making it less nucleophilic than compound (i).

In compound (iii) (triisopropylamine), the three isopropyl groups are highly bulky and cause severe steric crowding around the nitrogen atom. This effectively shields the lone pair, making it very difficult for the nitrogen to act as a nucleophile.

Therefore, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is $N,N$-dimethylaniline, which is a tertiary aromatic amine.

The reagent mixture of $\text{NaNO}_2$ and $\text{HCl}$ generates nitrous acid ($\text{HNO}_2$), which produces the nitrosonium ion ($\text{NO}^+$) as an electrophile.

Tertiary aromatic amines undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution when treated with nitrous acid. The $-\text{N(CH}_3)_2$ group is strongly ring-activating and directs the incoming electrophile to the ortho and para positions.

Due to significant steric hindrance at the ortho position, the electrophile ($\text{NO}^+$) attacks the para position predominantly.

Thus, the major product formed is $p$-nitroso-$N,N$-dimethylaniline.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant, N-methylaniline ($\text{Ph}-\text{NH}-\text{CH}_3$), is a secondary aromatic amine.

The reagent mixture of $\text{NaNO}_2$ and $\text{HCl}$ generates nitrous acid ($\text{HNO}_2$), which further produces the nitrosonium ion ($\text{NO}^+$) as an electrophile.

$\text{NaNO}_2 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_2 + \text{NaCl}$

$\text{HNO}_2 + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{O}^+-\text{NO} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NO}^+$

Secondary amines react with the nitrosonium ion to undergo N-nitrosation, forming N-nitrosamines as yellow oily liquids. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom of the secondary amine attacks the $\text{NO}^+$ ion, followed by the loss of a proton.

$\text{Ph}-\text{NH}-\text{CH}_3 + \text{NO}^+ \rightarrow \text{Ph}-\text{N}^+(\text{H})(\text{CH}_3)-\text{NO} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}^+} \text{Ph}-\text{N}(\text{NO})-\text{CH}_3$

The final major product is N-methyl-N-nitrosoaniline, which corresponds to the structure in option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The first step involves the reaction of aniline with $NaNO_2$ and $HCl$, which is a diazotization reaction. This produces benzene diazonium chloride ($C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^-$).

In the second step, benzene diazonium chloride is treated with $CuBr$. This is the Sandmeyer reaction, where the diazonium group is replaced by a bromine atom, with the release of nitrogen gas.

The final major product is bromobenzene ($C_6H_5Br$).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the starting material (?) in the given reaction sequence:

? $\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) Vinyl lithium, then } \mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O}^+, \Delta]{\text{(i) CuCN}}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two main steps to form phenyl vinyl ketone.

Step 1: The starting material reacts with $\text{CuCN}$. Among the given options, the benzenediazonium cation (Option A) undergoes the Sandmeyer reaction with $\text{CuCN}$ to form benzonitrile ($\text{Ph-CN}$).
$\text{Ph-N}_2^+ + \text{CuCN} \rightarrow \text{Ph-CN} + \text{N}_2$

Step 2: Benzonitrile reacts with vinyl lithium ($\text{CH}_2\text{=CH-Li}$). The nucleophilic vinyl group attacks the electrophilic carbon of the nitrile, forming an imine salt intermediate.
$\text{Ph-CN} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH-Li} \rightarrow \text{Ph-C(=NLi)-CH=CH}_2$

Step 3: Acidic hydrolysis ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+, \Delta$) of the imine salt converts it into the corresponding ketone, which is phenyl vinyl ketone (the given product).
$\text{Ph-C(=NLi)-CH=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \text{Ph-C(=O)-CH=CH}_2$

Thus, the correct starting material is the benzenediazonium cation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two steps:

Step 1: Aniline reacts with a mixture of $\text{NaNO}_2$ and $\text{HCl}$ at low temperatures ($0-5^\circ\text{C}$) to undergo diazotization. This forms benzene diazonium chloride.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2, \text{HCl}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^-$

Step 2: The benzene diazonium chloride is then treated with copper(I) bromide ($\text{CuBr}$). This is a Sandmeyer reaction, where the diazonium group is replaced by a bromide ion, releasing nitrogen gas.
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{CuBr}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Br} + \text{N}_2 \uparrow$

The final major product is bromobenzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction, assuming water is continuously removed to drive the equilibrium:

+
questions.solution:
The reaction involves a primary amine and a ketone (acetone) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($H^+$).

When a primary amine reacts with a ketone under acidic conditions, it undergoes a nucleophilic addition-elimination reaction (condensation) to form an imine (also known as a Schiff base).

The reaction proceeds via the formation of a hemiaminal intermediate, which then loses a molecule of water to form the carbon-nitrogen double bond of the imine.

The general reaction is: $R-NH_2 + R'_2C=O \xrightarrow{H^+} R-N=CR'_2 + H_2O$

In this specific case, the primary amino group ($-NH_2$) on the tetrahydronaphthalene derivative reacts with acetone ($CH_3COCH_3$) to form an isopropylideneamino group ($-N=C(CH_3)_2$).

The continuous removal of water drives the equilibrium forward according to Le Chatelier's principle, maximizing the yield of the imine product.

Option (A) represents an elimination product.
Option (B) represents the correct imine product.
Option (C) represents a secondary amine, which would require a reducing agent (reductive amination).
Option (D) represents an amide, which would require an acylating agent like an acid chloride or anhydride.

Therefore, the major product is the imine shown in option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Isopentyl nitrite acts as a source of $\mathrm{NO}^{+}$ ions, reacting with primary amines to generate diazonium cations. Predict the major product of the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The reaction of anthranilic acid (2-aminobenzoic acid) with isopentyl nitrite (a source of $\text{NO}^{+}$) converts the primary amine group into a diazonium salt, forming the zwitterionic intermediate benzenediazonium-2-carboxylate.

Upon heating, benzenediazonium-2-carboxylate undergoes simultaneous loss of $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{CO}_2$ to generate benzyne, a highly reactive intermediate.

Benzyne then undergoes a [4+2] cycloaddition (Diels-Alder reaction) with furan. In this reaction, furan acts as the diene ($4\pi$ component) and benzyne acts as the dienophile ($2\pi$ component). The addition occurs across the 2,5-positions of furan.

The resulting product is the bridged bicyclic compound 1,4-epoxy-1,4-dihydronaphthalene, which corresponds to the structure given in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a basic dye?
options: Alizarin
Aniline yellow
Congo red
Indigo
questions.solution:
Basic dyes are salts of colored organic bases containing amino groups ($-NH_2$, $-NHR$, $-NR_2$). In aqueous solution, they form colored cations.

Aniline yellow (p-aminoazobenzene) contains an amino group, which makes it basic in nature. It gets protonated in an acidic medium to form a colored cation, hence it is classified as a basic dye.

Alizarin is a mordant dye.

Congo red is a direct dye (and an acidic dye due to the presence of sulfonic acid groups).

Indigo is a vat dye.

Answer: Aniline yellow

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reaction sequences is NOT a suitable synthesis for benzylamine?
questions.solution:
The target molecule is benzylamine, which has the structure $C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$.

In option (A), benzyl bromide ($C_6H_5CH_2Br$) reacts with $NaCN$ to undergo nucleophilic substitution, forming benzyl cyanide ($C_6H_5CH_2CN$). Subsequent reduction of benzyl cyanide with $LiAlH_4$ yields 2-phenylethanamine ($C_6H_5CH_2CH_2NH_2$), which contains one more carbon atom than benzylamine. Thus, this is not a suitable synthesis.

In option (B), benzamide ($C_6H_5CONH_2$) is reduced by $LiAlH_4$ to form benzylamine ($C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$).

In option (C), 2-phenylacetamide ($C_6H_5CH_2CONH_2$) undergoes Hoffmann bromamide degradation in the presence of $Br_2$ and $NaOH$ to form benzylamine ($C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$).

In option (D), benzyl bromide ($C_6H_5CH_2Br$) reacts with a large excess of ammonia ($NH_3$) via nucleophilic substitution to yield benzylamine ($C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$) as the major product.

Therefore, the reaction sequence in option (A) is not a suitable synthesis for benzylamine.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two main steps:

1. Diazotization: Aniline reacts with $NaNO_2$ and $HCl$ (usually at $0-5^{\circ}C$) to form benzene diazonium chloride ($C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^-$).

2. Azo Coupling: The benzene diazonium ion acts as a weak electrophile and reacts with N,N-dimethylaniline. The $-N(CH_3)_2$ group is a strongly activating, ortho/para-directing group. Due to significant steric hindrance at the ortho position, the electrophilic attack occurs predominantly at the para position of the N,N-dimethylaniline ring.

The resulting major product is p-(dimethylamino)azobenzene, which is a yellow azo dye.

The structure corresponds to option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major organic product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence is an example of the Gabriel phthalimide synthesis, which is used for the preparation of primary amines.

In the first step, the phthalimide anion acts as a nucleophile and undergoes an $S_N2$ reaction with benzyl bromide. The nitrogen atom attacks the electrophilic benzylic carbon, displacing the bromide ion to form N-benzylphthalimide.

In the second step, N-benzylphthalimide is subjected to acidic hydrolysis ($H^+$, $H_2O$, heat). This process cleaves the two carbon-nitrogen bonds of the imide, resulting in the formation of phthalic acid and a primary amine.

The primary amine formed in this case is benzylamine ($C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$).

Therefore, the major organic product is benzylamine.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the compounds $\mathrm{B}$, $\mathrm{X}$, and $\mathrm{R}$ respectively in the following sequences of reactions:

(i) $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{MgBr} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{ClCN}} \mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{B}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{NaOH}]{\mathrm{I}_{2}} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Ag}} \mathrm{Y}$
(iii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{HCl}]{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}} \mathrm{P} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CuCN}} \mathrm{Q} \xrightarrow{+4\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{R}$
options: $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$, $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{I}$, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CONH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$, $\mathrm{CHI}_{3}$, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$
questions.solution:
In sequence (i), the reaction of ethyl magnesium bromide with cyanogen chloride yields propanenitrile (A).

$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{MgBr} + \mathrm{ClCN} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CN} + \mathrm{MgBrCl}$

Hydrolysis of propanenitrile in an acidic medium yields propanoic acid (B).

$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CN} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$

In sequence (ii), acetone undergoes the iodoform reaction with iodine and sodium hydroxide to form iodoform (X) and sodium acetate.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{NaOH}]{\mathrm{I}_{2}} \mathrm{CHI}_{3} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COONa}$

Heating iodoform with silver powder yields acetylene (Y).

$2\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Ag}} \mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{CH}$

In sequence (iii), aniline undergoes diazotization with sodium nitrite and hydrochloric acid to form benzenediazonium chloride (P).

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{HCl}]{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$

Reaction of benzenediazonium chloride with cuprous cyanide (Sandmeyer reaction) yields benzonitrile (Q).

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CuCN}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CN}$

Reduction of benzonitrile yields benzylamine (R).

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CN} \xrightarrow{+4\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$

Thus, $\mathrm{B}$ is $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$, $\mathrm{X}$ is $\mathrm{CHI}_{3}$, and $\mathrm{R}$ is $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{COOH}$, $\mathrm{CHI}_{3}$, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product [D] in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The starting material, benzanilide, has an activated benzene ring attached to the $-NH-$ group and a deactivated ring attached to the $-CO-$ group. Electrophilic aromatic substitution will preferentially occur on the activated ring.

Step 1: Friedel-Crafts alkylation with $CH_3CH_2CH_2Cl$ and $AlCl_3$ involves the formation of a primary $n$-propyl carbocation, which rearranges to a more stable secondary isopropyl carbocation, $(CH_3)_2CH^+$. This electrophile attacks the sterically less hindered para position of the activated ring to form [A], $N$-(4-isopropylphenyl)benzamide.

Step 2: Acidic hydrolysis of [A] with $H_3O^+$ cleaves the amide bond to produce [B], 4-isopropylaniline, and benzoic acid.

Step 3: Diazotization of the primary aromatic amine [B] with $NaNO_2/HCl$ at $0^\circ-5^\circ\text{C}$ yields the diazonium salt [C], 4-isopropylbenzenediazonium chloride.

Step 4: Azo coupling of [C] with $N,N$-dimethylaniline occurs at the para position of the highly activated $N,N$-dimethylaniline ring. This forms the major product [D], which is an azo dye named 4-(4-isopropylphenylazo)-$N,N$-dimethylaniline.

The structure of [D] is $(CH_3)_2CH-C_6H_4-N=N-C_6H_4-N(CH_3)_2$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Acetamide behaves as both a weak acid and a weak base.
All of the above statements are true.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
At room temperature, formyl chloride is unstable and is present in the form of $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$.
questions.solution:
Acetamide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CONH}_{2}$) is amphoteric in nature. It acts as a weak base because the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized over the carbonyl group, and it acts as a weak acid because the hydrogen atom attached to the nitrogen can be removed by a strong base.

Reduction of acetamide with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ yields ethylamine ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$).

Formyl chloride ($\mathrm{HCOCl}$) is highly unstable at room temperature and readily decomposes into carbon monoxide ($\mathrm{CO}$) and hydrogen chloride ($\mathrm{HCl}$).

Since all the given statements are correct, the option stating that all statements are true is the correct choice.

Answer: All of the above statements are true.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acid amides do not undergo the typical reactions of the carbonyl ($\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$) group because:
options: it is amphoteric
it is a weak base
it is a weak acid
its carbonyl carbon is not electron deficient
questions.solution:
In acid amides ($R-CO-NH_2$), the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is in conjugation with the $\pi$ electrons of the carbonyl group.

This results in resonance: $R-C(=O)-NH_2 \leftrightarrow R-C(-O^-)=N^+H_2$

Due to this delocalization of electrons, the electron density on the carbonyl carbon increases significantly. As a result, the carbonyl carbon loses its electrophilic character and is no longer electron deficient.

Since typical reactions of the carbonyl group (like nucleophilic addition) require an electron-deficient carbonyl carbon, acid amides do not undergo these typical reactions.

Answer: its carbonyl carbon is not electron deficient

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the three amines shown below:

           

Which of the following statements regarding their relative basicity is correct?
options: Aniline is the strongest base, and cyclohexylamine is the weakest base.
4-Nitroaniline is the strongest base, and cyclohexylamine is the weakest base.
Cyclohexylamine is the strongest base, and 4-nitroaniline is the weakest base.
Cyclohexylamine is the strongest base, and aniline is the weakest base.
questions.solution:
The basicity of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

Cyclohexylamine is an aliphatic amine. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is localized and not involved in any resonance. Therefore, it is readily available for protonation, making cyclohexylamine the strongest base among the three.

Aniline is an aromatic amine. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized into the benzene ring through resonance. This delocalization reduces the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making aniline a weaker base than cyclohexylamine.

In 4-nitroaniline, the $-NO_2$ group is present at the para position. The $-NO_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group and exerts both $-I$ (inductive) and $-R$ (resonance) effects. This further decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom compared to aniline, making the lone pair even less available for protonation. Thus, 4-nitroaniline is the weakest base.

Therefore, cyclohexylamine is the strongest base, and 4-nitroaniline is the weakest base.

Answer: Cyclohexylamine is the strongest base, and 4-nitroaniline is the weakest base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves a secondary amine reacting with a ketone (acetone) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($H^+$).

When a secondary amine reacts with a ketone under acidic conditions, it undergoes nucleophilic addition followed by dehydration to form an enamine. The steps are as follows:

1. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom of the secondary amine attacks the protonated carbonyl carbon of acetone, forming a hemiaminal intermediate.
2. Protonation of the hydroxyl group and subsequent loss of a water molecule leads to the formation of an iminium ion intermediate. In this case, the iminium ion is formed between the nitrogen and the central carbon of the acetone moiety: $R_2N^+=C(CH_3)_2$.
3. To regain neutrality and form a stable product, a proton is removed from an $\alpha$-carbon. The $\alpha$-carbons are the methyl groups derived from acetone.
4. Deprotonation of one of these methyl groups results in the formation of a carbon-carbon double bond, yielding the enamine: $R_2N-C(CH_3)=CH_2$.

Looking at the given options:
- Option (A) correctly shows the enamine product where the nitrogen is bonded to an isopropenyl group ($-C(CH_3)=CH_2$).
- Option (B) shows an imine, which would only form if the starting material was a primary amine.
- Option (C) shows a double bond within the tetralin ring, which is incorrect as the carbonyl carbon was on acetone.
- Option (D) shows an iminium ion, which is a charged intermediate, not the final neutral major product.

Therefore, the major product is the enamine shown in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of basic strength of ethylamine, diethylamine, and triethylamine in chlorobenzene is:
options: $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N}$
$(\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH} < \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
$(\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N} < \mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH}$
questions.solution:
Chlorobenzene is a non-polar, aprotic solvent.

In non-polar solvents or in the gas phase, the basic strength of amines is determined solely by the +I (inductive) effect of the alkyl groups, as there is no solvation effect (hydrogen bonding) to stabilize the conjugate acid.

The +I effect increases with the number of alkyl groups attached to the nitrogen atom, which increases the electron density on the nitrogen atom and its ability to donate the lone pair.

Therefore, the basic strength increases in the order: primary amine < secondary amine < tertiary amine.

The correct order is $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH} < (\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{3}\mathrm{N}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of increasing basicity for the following compounds:

(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
(II)
(III)
options: (I) $\approx$ (III) < (II)
(I) < (II) < (III)
(II) < (III) < (I)
(III) < (I) < (II)
questions.solution:
Compound (I) is ethylamine ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$), an aliphatic amine. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom is localized, and the +I effect of the ethyl group increases the electron density on nitrogen, making it a moderately strong base.

Compound (II) is acetamidine ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}(=\mathrm{NH})\mathrm{NH}_{2}$). Protonation occurs at the imine nitrogen ($=\mathrm{NH}$). The resulting conjugate acid is highly stabilized by resonance between two equivalent structures, distributing the positive charge equally over two nitrogen atoms. This makes acetamidine a very strong base.

Compound (III) is acetamide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}(=\mathrm{O})\mathrm{NH}_{2}$). The lone pair on the nitrogen atom is strongly delocalized into the highly electronegative oxygen atom of the carbonyl group via resonance. This significantly reduces the availability of the lone pair for protonation, making it a very weak base.

Therefore, the correct order of increasing basicity is (III) < (I) < (II).

Answer: (III) < (I) < (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
Electrophilic aromatic bromination of nitrobenzene occurs at the meta position due to the meta-directing nature of the $-NO_2$ group, yielding m-bromonitrobenzene.

Catalytic hydrogenation using $H_2/Ni$ reduces the $-NO_2$ group to an $-NH_2$ group, forming m-bromoaniline. Diazotization with $NaNO_2$ and $H^+$ converts the amine into a diazonium salt.

The Sandmeyer reaction with $CuCN$ replaces the diazonium group with a cyanide group, producing m-bromobenzonitrile.

Reduction of the nitrile group with $LiAlH_4$ followed by hydrolysis yields a primary amine. The $-CN$ group is converted to $-CH_2NH_2$, resulting in m-bromobenzylamine as the final product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following reagents can reduce nitrobenzene to aniline?
(I) $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni}$
(II) $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$
(III) $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{NaOH}$
(IV) $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$
options: (I), (II) and (IV)
(I), (II) and (III)
Only (II)
(I) and (II)
questions.solution:
Nitrobenzene is reduced to aniline by catalytic hydrogenation using $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in the presence of finely divided $\mathrm{Ni}$, $\mathrm{Pd}$, or $\mathrm{Pt}$.

Nitrobenzene is also reduced to aniline by active metals in acidic medium, such as $\mathrm{Sn}/\mathrm{HCl}$ or $\mathrm{Fe}/\mathrm{HCl}$.

Reduction of nitrobenzene with $\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{NaOH}$ (alkaline medium) yields hydrazobenzene.

Reduction of nitrobenzene with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ yields azobenzene.

Therefore, only $\mathrm{H}_{2}/\mathrm{Ni}$ and $\mathrm{Sn}/\mathrm{HCl}$ can reduce nitrobenzene to aniline.

Answer: (I) and (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the reaction that will NOT yield the product shown.

(I)

(II)

(III)

(IV)
options: Only (I)
Only (II)
Only (III)
Only (IV)
questions.solution:
Reaction (I) represents the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of an amide, which correctly yields a carboxylic acid (butanoic acid).

Reaction (II) shows the reduction of an amide using $LiAlH_4$. The reduction of a primary amide with $LiAlH_4$ yields a primary amine, not an alcohol. Thus, butanamide will reduce to butan-1-amine ($CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2NH_2$), not butan-1-ol. This reaction will NOT yield the product shown.

Reaction (III) represents the base-catalyzed hydrolysis of an amide, which correctly yields a carboxylate ion (butanoate ion).

Reaction (IV) is the Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. A primary amide reacts with $Br_2$ and $NaOH$ to form a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original amide. Butanamide correctly yields propan-1-amine.

Therefore, only reaction (II) does not yield the given product.

Answer: Only (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of decreasing basic strength for the following aliphatic primary amines:

(I)
(II)
(III)
options: (I) $=$ (II) $\equiv$ (III)
(III) > (II) > (I)
(I) > (II) $\approx$ (III)
(I) > (II) > (III)
questions.solution:
The basic strength of an amine depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation. Electron-donating groups ($+I$ effect) increase basicity, while electron-withdrawing groups ($-I$ effect) decrease basicity.

In compound (I), the alkyl group (butyl) exerts a $+I$ effect, increasing the electron density on the nitrogen atom.

In compound (II), the group attached to the $-\text{CH}_2-\text{NH}_2$ group contains an $sp^2$ hybridized carbon. Since an $sp^2$ carbon is more electronegative than an $sp^3$ carbon, it exerts a $-I$ effect, which decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom.

In compound (III), the group attached to the $-\text{CH}_2-\text{NH}_2$ group contains an $sp$ hybridized carbon. An $sp$ carbon is more electronegative than an $sp^2$ carbon, so it exerts a stronger $-I$ effect, further decreasing the electron density on the nitrogen atom.

The order of electronegativity of carbon atoms is $sp > sp^2 > sp^3$. Therefore, the electron-withdrawing nature increases in the order (I) < (II) < (III).

Consequently, the availability of the lone pair on nitrogen decreases, and the basic strength follows the order: (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements is correct regarding the basicity of the given primary amines?

(i)

(ii)
options: $\mathrm{(i)} < \mathrm{(ii)}$ because of the difference in the nature of the $\beta$-carbon.
$\mathrm{(ii)} > \mathrm{(i)}$ because $\mathrm{(ii)}$ is an aliphatic amine.
$\mathrm{(i)} > \mathrm{(ii)}$ because $\mathrm{(i)}$ is an aromatic amine.
Both are equally basic because both are $1^{\circ}$ amines.
questions.solution:
The basicity of an amine depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

In both compounds, the $-\mathrm{NH_2}$ group is attached to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon (the $\alpha$-carbon).

In benzylamine $\mathrm{(i)}$, the $\alpha$-carbon is attached to a phenyl ring. The carbon atom of the phenyl ring directly attached to the $\alpha$-carbon (the $\beta$-carbon) is $sp^2$ hybridized. Since $sp^2$ hybridized carbons are more electronegative than $sp^3$ hybridized carbons, the phenyl group exerts an electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect). This decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making it less basic.

In cyclohexylmethylamine $\mathrm{(ii)}$, the $\alpha$-carbon is attached to a cyclohexyl ring. The $\beta$-carbon here is $sp^3$ hybridized. Alkyl groups like the cyclohexyl group exert an electron-donating inductive effect ($+I$ effect), which increases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making the lone pair more available for protonation.

Therefore, cyclohexylmethylamine $\mathrm{(ii)}$ is more basic than benzylamine $\mathrm{(i)}$. The difference in basicity is primarily due to the difference in the hybridization and nature of the $\beta$-carbon.

Answer: $\mathrm{(i)} < \mathrm{(ii)}$ because of the difference in the nature of the $\beta$-carbon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The correct order of decreasing basic strength for the following amines is:
(i) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
(iii) $(\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5})_{2}\mathrm{NH}$
(iv) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{11}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
options: (iv) > (ii) > (iii) > (i)
(ii) > (i) > (iii) > (iv)
(iv) > (iii) > (ii) > (i)
(iv) > (ii) > (i) > (iii)
questions.solution:
Basic strength of amines depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

In cyclohexylamine (iv), the lone pair on nitrogen is localized and the cyclohexyl group exerts a +I effect, increasing electron density on nitrogen. It is the most basic.

In benzylamine (ii), the $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is attached to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon, so the lone pair is localized. However, the phenyl group exerts a -I effect, making it slightly less basic than cyclohexylamine.

In aniline (i), the lone pair on nitrogen is delocalized into the benzene ring due to resonance, making it less available for protonation. Thus, it is less basic than aliphatic amines.

In diphenylamine (iii), the lone pair is delocalized over two benzene rings, making it the least available for protonation. It is the least basic.

Therefore, the correct order of decreasing basic strength is (iv) > (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (iv) > (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To obtain the best yield of the azo dye when benzenediazonium chloride is coupled with phenol, the pH of the reaction medium should be maintained at
options: around 8
around 10
around 4
questions.solution:
The coupling reaction of benzenediazonium chloride with phenol is an electrophilic aromatic substitution where the diazonium ion acts as the electrophile.

Phenol is converted into the phenoxide ion in an alkaline medium, which is a much stronger nucleophile and highly reactive towards electrophilic substitution. However, if the medium is too highly alkaline (pH $> 10$), the diazonium ion reacts with hydroxide ions to form diazohydroxide and subsequently the diazotate ion, which are not electrophilic and do not undergo coupling.

Therefore, the reaction is carried out in a mildly alkaline medium with a pH of $9$ to $10$ to maintain an optimal concentration of both the reactive phenoxide ion and the electrophilic diazonium ion. Thus, the pH should be maintained around $10$.

Answer: around 10

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of basic strength for the following compounds:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
(V)
questions.solution:
The basic strength of nitrogenous compounds depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

1. Compound (II) is piperidine, a secondary aliphatic amine where the nitrogen atom is $sp^3$ hybridized. It has no electron-withdrawing groups, making its lone pair highly available. Thus, it is the most basic.

2. Compounds (IV) morpholine and (V) thiomorpholine are also secondary aliphatic amines but contain electronegative heteroatoms (oxygen and sulfur, respectively) at the 4-position. These heteroatoms exert an electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect), which decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom, making them less basic than piperidine. Since oxygen is more electronegative than sulfur, the $-I$ effect of oxygen is stronger than that of sulfur. Therefore, morpholine (IV) is less basic than thiomorpholine (V). This gives the order: (II) > (V) > (IV).

3. Compound (I) is pyridine. The nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized, meaning its lone pair resides in an $sp^2$ orbital. An $sp^2$ orbital has more s-character ($33.3\%$) compared to an $sp^3$ orbital ($25\%$), so the nucleus holds the lone pair more tightly, making it less available for protonation than in aliphatic amines. Thus, pyridine is less basic than morpholine. This updates the order to: (II) > (V) > (IV) > (I).

4. Compound (III) is N-acetylpiperidine, which is an amide. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom is highly delocalized into the adjacent carbonyl group via resonance. This delocalization makes the lone pair practically unavailable for protonation, making amides the least basic among the given compounds.

Combining all these factors, the correct decreasing order of basic strength is (II) > (V) > (IV) > (I) > (III).

Answer: (II) > (V) > (IV) > (I) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ephedrine is a secondary amine widely used in cold and allergy medications. It is typically formulated as its hydrochloride salt rather than as the free amine because:
options: the free amine is unstable and easily oxidized by air, while the salt is resistant to atmospheric oxidation.
the free amine is insoluble in water, while the salt is soluble in water.
all of the above reasons are correct.
the free amine has an unpleasant smell, while its salt is odourless.
questions.solution:
Amines are typically formulated as their hydrochloride salts in pharmaceutical preparations for several reasons.

First, free amines are often susceptible to oxidation by atmospheric oxygen, making them unstable over time, whereas their protonated salts are highly resistant to such oxidation.

Second, free amines generally have poor solubility in water, which hinders their absorption and formulation in aqueous media. Converting them into ionic hydrochloride salts significantly increases their water solubility.

Third, free amines typically possess a strong, unpleasant, fishy or ammonia-like odour, whereas their solid salts are odourless.

Since all these factors make the hydrochloride salt more suitable for medication, all the given reasons are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the product(s) $P$ formed in the following reaction:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2} \xrightarrow[0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}, \mathrm{HCl}} P$
options: Reaction is not possible
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl}$
Both $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl}$
questions.solution:
The reaction of a primary aliphatic amine with $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ produces an unstable aliphatic diazonium salt.

This salt rapidly decomposes to release nitrogen gas and form a primary carbocation:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}, \mathrm{HCl}} [\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}] \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+} + \mathrm{N}_{2} + \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$

The primary carbocation ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$) can react with water (from the aqueous medium) to form propan-1-ol ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$).

It can also undergo a 1,2-hydride shift to form a more stable secondary carbocation ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}^{+}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$).

The secondary carbocation can react with chloride ions present in the solution to form 2-chloropropane ($\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}$).

Therefore, a mixture of products is obtained, including both $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2}\mathrm{CHCl}$.

Answer: Both $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ and $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the reagent $\mathrm{X}$ in the given reaction:
$\mathrm{RCOOH} + \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{RNH}_{2}$
options: Neither $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ nor $\mathrm{HN}_{3}$
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
Either $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ or $\mathrm{HN}_{3}$
$\mathrm{HN}_{3}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the Schmidt reaction.

In this reaction, a carboxylic acid reacts with hydrazoic acid ($\mathrm{HN}_{3}$) in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid to form a primary amine with one carbon atom less than the original carboxylic acid.

$\mathrm{RCOOH} + \mathrm{HN}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{RNH}_{2} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} + \mathrm{N}_{2}$

Therefore, the reagent $\mathrm{X}$ is $\mathrm{HN}_{3}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{HN}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents does not reduce nitrobenzene ($\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$) to aniline?
options: $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$
$\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{SnCl}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{HCl}$
questions.solution:
Reduction of nitrobenzene with metals in acidic medium such as $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{HCl}$, $\mathrm{SnCl}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$, or $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{HCl}$ yields aniline.

However, when nitrobenzene is reduced with lithium aluminium hydride ($\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$), it undergoes a bimolecular reduction to form azobenzene ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}$), not aniline. Note that $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ reduces aliphatic nitro compounds to primary amines, but it couples aromatic nitro compounds to form azo compounds.

Therefore, $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ does not reduce nitrobenzene to aniline.

Answer: $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following amines undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution when treated with nitrous acid at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$?
options: None of these
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NHCH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
Primary aromatic amines react with nitrous acid at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to form diazonium salts.

Secondary aromatic amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosoamines, which separate as a yellow oil.

Tertiary aromatic amines, such as $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$, have no hydrogen atoms on the nitrogen atom. The highly activated benzene ring undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution with the nitrosonium ion ($\mathrm{NO}^{+}$) at the para position to form p-nitroso-N,N-dimethylaniline.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product $\mathrm{Z}$ in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reactant shown is pentane-1,5-diamine dihydrochloride, $\text{Cl}^-\text{H}_3\text{N}^+-(\text{CH}_2)_5-\text{N}^+\text{H}_3\text{Cl}^-$.

When heated, it undergoes an intramolecular cyclization reaction. One of the amino groups acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbon atom at the other end of the chain and displacing the second amino group as ammonia (which leaves as ammonium chloride).

The cyclization of the 5-carbon chain along with the nitrogen atom results in the formation of a 6-membered saturated heterocyclic ring.

The product formed is piperidine.

Structure (B) corresponds to piperidine.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Benzamide and benzylamine can be distinguished by:
options: both cold dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$
cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$
cold dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$
$\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, followed by $\beta$-naphthol
questions.solution:
Benzamide ($\mathrm{PhCONH_2}$) is a neutral compound and does not dissolve in cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$ or cold dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$.

Benzylamine ($\mathrm{PhCH_2NH_2}$) is a primary aliphatic amine and is basic in nature. It reacts with cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$ to form a water-soluble salt, benzylammonium chloride.

$\mathrm{PhCH_2NH_2} + \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{PhCH_2NH_3^+Cl^-}$ (soluble)

Since benzylamine dissolves in cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$ while benzamide does not, cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$ can be used to distinguish between them.

Answer: cold dilute $\mathrm{HCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct increasing order of basic strength for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CN}$
options: (iv) < (ii) < (iii) < (i)
(iv) < (iii) < (i) < (ii)
(iv) < (i) < (iii) < (ii)
(iv) < (iii) < (ii) < (i)
questions.solution:
The basic strength of nitrogenous compounds depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation. This availability is influenced by the hybridization of the nitrogen atom and whether the lone pair is involved in resonance or aromaticity.

In piperidine (ii), the nitrogen atom is $sp^3$ hybridized. The lone pair is localized in an $sp^3$ orbital and is readily available for protonation, making it the most basic among the given compounds.

In pyridine (i), the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized. The lone pair is localized in an $sp^2$ orbital and is not part of the aromatic $\pi$-system. Since an $sp^2$ hybridized nitrogen is more electronegative than an $sp^3$ hybridized nitrogen, pyridine is less basic than piperidine.

In pyrrole (iii), the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized, and its lone pair resides in a $p$ orbital, participating in the aromatic $6\pi$ electron system. Protonation of this nitrogen would disrupt the aromaticity of the ring, making pyrrole a very weak base.

In acetonitrile (iv), $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CN}$, the nitrogen atom is $sp$ hybridized. The lone pair is in an $sp$ orbital, which has $50\%$ s-character. This high s-character makes the nitrogen atom highly electronegative, holding the lone pair very tightly and making it the least available for protonation. Thus, it is the least basic.

Therefore, the increasing order of basic strength is (iv) < (iii) < (i) < (ii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds does not react with nitrous acid?
options: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NHCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$
None of these
questions.solution:
Primary aromatic amines like $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ react with nitrous acid at low temperatures to form diazonium salts.

Secondary aromatic amines like $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{NHCH}_{3}$ react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosoamines, which separate as yellow oily liquids.

Tertiary aromatic amines like $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$ react with nitrous acid to undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution, yielding p-nitroso derivatives (e.g., p-nitroso-N,N-dimethylaniline).

Since all the given compounds react with nitrous acid, none of them is unreactive.

Answer: None of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true regarding the relative basic character of pyridine and pyrrole?
options: Pyridine is less basic because it is a tertiary amine.
Both are equally basic.
Pyridine is more basic because its non-bonding electron pair is not a part of the aromatic sextet.
Pyrrole is more basic because its non-bonding electron pair occupies an $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ orbital.
questions.solution:
In pyridine, the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized and its lone pair of electrons is present in an $sp^2$ hybridized orbital. This non-bonding electron pair lies in the plane of the ring and does not participate in the delocalized $\pi$ electron system of the aromatic ring. Therefore, the lone pair is readily available for protonation, making pyridine a base.

In pyrrole, the nitrogen atom is also $sp^2$ hybridized, but its lone pair of electrons resides in an unhybridized $p$ orbital. This lone pair participates in the continuous delocalization with the four carbon $\pi$ electrons to form the aromatic sextet of the ring. As a result, the electron pair is not readily available for donation to a proton. Protonation of pyrrole would destroy its aromaticity, making it a very weak base.

Thus, pyridine is much more basic than pyrrole because its non-bonding electron pair is not a part of the aromatic sextet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is NOT an oxidation product of a primary amine?
options: None of these
A nitroso compound
A nitro compound
A hydroxylamine
questions.solution:
Primary amines can undergo oxidation to form various products depending on the nature of the oxidizing agent.

Oxidation of a primary amine ($R-NH_2$) with Caro's acid ($H_2SO_5$) or hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$) yields a hydroxylamine ($R-NHOH$).

Further oxidation of the hydroxylamine produces a nitroso compound ($R-NO$).

Strong oxidizing agents like potassium permanganate ($KMnO_4$) or peroxy acids can oxidize primary amines all the way to nitro compounds ($R-NO_2$).

Since hydroxylamines, nitroso compounds, and nitro compounds are all possible oxidation products of primary amines, none of the given options is incorrect.

Answer: None of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following methods can be used for eliminating the nitrogen atom of an amine present outside a ring?
options: Emde degradation
Hofmann elimination
Both Hofmann and Cope eliminations
Cope elimination
questions.solution:
Hofmann elimination involves the exhaustive methylation of an amine to form a quaternary ammonium salt, followed by heating with moist silver oxide ($\text{Ag}_{2}\text{O}/\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$) to yield an alkene and a tertiary amine. This effectively eliminates the nitrogen atom from the carbon chain.

Cope elimination involves the oxidation of a tertiary amine to an amine oxide, which upon heating undergoes a syn-elimination to form an alkene and a hydroxylamine, also eliminating the nitrogen atom from the carbon chain.

Both methods are standard procedures for eliminating the nitrogen atom of an amine present outside a ring. Emde degradation, on the other hand, is typically used for cleaving carbon-nitrogen bonds when the nitrogen is part of a ring and Hofmann elimination fails.

Answer: Both Hofmann and Cope eliminations

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:



(i) $\mathrm{Ac}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(ii) $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(iii) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}$
(iv) $\mathrm{Br}_{2} + \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$
(v) $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} + \mathrm{HCl}$
(vi) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{PO}_{2}$
(vii) $\mathrm{Fe} + \mathrm{HCl}$
(viii) $\mathrm{OH}^{-} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
questions.solution:
Reaction of aniline with $Ac_2O$ yields acetanilide, protecting the amino group and reducing its activating effect.

Nitration of acetanilide with $HNO_3$ and $H_2SO_4$ gives p-nitroacetanilide as the major product due to steric hindrance at the ortho position.

Acidic hydrolysis using $H_3O^+$ removes the acetyl group to yield p-nitroaniline.

Bromination with $Br_2$ and $NaHCO_3$ substitutes bromine at both ortho positions relative to the strongly activating $-NH_2$ group, forming 2,6-dibromo-4-nitroaniline.

Reaction with $NaNO_2$ and $HCl$ converts the primary amine into a diazonium salt, forming 2,6-dibromo-4-nitrobenzenediazonium chloride.

Treatment with $H_3PO_2$ reduces the diazonium group, replacing it with a hydrogen atom to produce 3,5-dibromonitrobenzene.

Reduction of the nitro group using $Fe$ and $HCl$ followed by basic workup with $OH^-/H_2O$ converts the nitro group to an amine, yielding 3,5-dibromoaniline as the final major product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves a primary amide reacting with $\text{NaOH}$ and $\text{Br}_2$. This is the standard reagent condition for the Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction.

In the Hofmann bromamide degradation, a primary amide ($R-\text{CONH}_2$) is converted into a primary amine ($R-\text{NH}_2$) with one less carbon atom. The carbonyl group ($-\text{CO}-$) is lost as carbonate.

The general reaction is:
$R-\text{CONH}_2 + \text{Br}_2 + 4\text{NaOH} \rightarrow R-\text{NH}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{NaBr} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

Applying this to the given reactant, 3,4-dimethoxybenzamide, the $-\text{CONH}_2$ group attached to the benzene ring is converted to an $-\text{NH}_2$ group. The rest of the molecule, including the two methoxy ($-\text{OMe}$) groups on the benzene ring, remains unaffected.

Therefore, the product formed is 3,4-dimethoxyaniline, which matches the structure given in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:



Reagents:
(i) $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$
(ii) $\mathrm{NaN}_{3}$
(iii) $\mathrm{Ag}_{2}\mathrm{O} / \Delta$
(iv) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(v)
(vi) LAH
options: None of these
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves the following steps:

1. Formation of Acid Chloride: The starting material is butanoic acid (a 4-carbon carboxylic acid). Reaction with thionyl chloride ($\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$) converts it into butanoyl chloride.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{SOCl}_2} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCl}$

2. Formation of Acyl Azide: Nucleophilic acyl substitution with sodium azide ($\mathrm{NaN}_{3}$) yields butanoyl azide.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COCl} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaN}_3} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CON}_3$

3. Curtius Rearrangement: Heating the acyl azide in the presence of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ causes the loss of nitrogen gas and rearrangement to form propyl isocyanate.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CON}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2N=C=O} + \mathrm{N}_2$

4. Hydrolysis: Hydrolysis of the isocyanate yields a primary amine with one less carbon atom than the original acid. Here, it forms propan-1-amine (propylamine).
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2N=C=O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2NH}_2 + \mathrm{CO}_2$

5. Amide Formation: The propylamine reacts with propanoic acid (the 3-carbon acid shown in step v) in the presence of pyridine to form an amide, N-propylpropanamide.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2NH}_2 + \mathrm{CH_3CH_2COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyridine}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CONHCH_2CH_2CH_3}$

6. Reduction: Finally, lithium aluminum hydride (LAH) reduces the carbonyl group of the amide to a methylene ($\mathrm{-CH_2-}$) group, yielding a secondary amine.
$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CONHCH_2CH_2CH_3} \xrightarrow{\text{LAH}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2NHCH_2CH_2CH_3}$

The final major product is dipropylamine, which corresponds to the structure in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence is the Graebe-Ullmann synthesis of carbazole.

Step 1: The starting material, 2-aminodiphenylamine, reacts with $\text{NaNO}_2$ and $\text{HCl}$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$. The primary amino group ($-\text{NH}_2$) undergoes diazotization to form a diazonium salt. The adjacent secondary amino group ($-\text{NH}-$ ) then attacks the diazonium group intramolecularly, leading to the formation of 1-phenyl-1H-benzotriazole.

Step 2: Upon heating ($\Delta$), 1-phenyl-1H-benzotriazole undergoes thermal decomposition. It loses a molecule of nitrogen gas ($\text{N}_2$) to form a reactive diradical intermediate, which subsequently undergoes intramolecular cyclization to yield carbazole.

The structure of carbazole corresponds to the compound shown in option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
Fluorene is deprotonated by one equivalent of BuLi to generate the fluorenyl anion.

The fluorenyl anion attacks one of the carbonyl groups of $N$-tosyloxyphthalimide, leading to ring opening and the formation of a 2-(9-fluorenylcarbonyl)-$N$-tosyloxybenzamide intermediate.

Under the basic conditions, this intermediate undergoes a Lossen rearrangement. The aryl group migrates to the nitrogen atom with the elimination of the tosylate group, yielding 2-(9-fluorenylcarbonyl)phenyl isocyanate.

The second equivalent of BuLi deprotonates the remaining acidic proton at the C9 position of the fluorenyl group to form a carbanion.

This carbanion undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic attack on the central carbon of the isocyanate group.

This cyclization forms a six-membered quinoline-2,4-dione ring, resulting in the formation of spiro[fluorene-9,3'-quinoline]-2',4'($1'H$)-dione as the major product.

Answer:
